primary_meaning: annihilation, complete destruction or nullification of being
secondary_meaning: the metaphysical state of absolute cessation of existence or consciousness
usage_notes: Used in philosophical contexts with 'anéantissement de', 'anéantissement du', spiritual traditions, mystical dissolution. Often paired with 'ego', 'être', 'illusions'
common_collocations: anéantissement de l'ego, anéantissement de soi, anéantissement total, anéantissement spirituel, état d'anéantissement
image_search_query: dissolution being annihilation spiritual emptiness void
image_context: Abstract representation of dissolution and spiritual emptiness symbolizing complete negation of self
example_1: L'anéantissement des illusions est nécessaire pour accéder à la réalité transcendante.
example_1_translation: The annihilation of illusions is necessary to access transcendent reality.
example_2: La doctrine bouddhiste du Nirvana enseigne l'anéantissement de la souffrance.
example_2_translation: The Buddhist doctrine of Nirvana teaches the annihilation of suffering.
example_3: Dans le mysticisme soufi, l'anéantissement du moi précède l'union divine.
example_3_translation: In Sufi mysticism, the annihilation of the self precedes divine union.
pronunciation_tips: Five syllables: a-ne-an-TIS-man. Stress on fourth: a-ne-an-TIS-man. Silent 't' in French often flows.
memory_hook: Anéantissement - from 'néant' (nothingness) + '-issement' (state) - the state of becoming nothing
related_words: néant, néantise, nihilisme, extinction, cessation, Nirvana
common_mistakes: Pronounced with too much emphasis on 'an'; avoid 'a-NAY-an-tis-man'

primary_meaning: aporia, a logical impasse or irresolvable contradiction in reasoning
secondary_meaning: in Socratic dialogue, the state of perplexity when assumptions prove false, used as a teaching method
usage_notes: Used in philosophical discussions, rhetoric. Common phrases: 'aporie fruitière', 'aporie dialectique', 'aboutir à une aporie'
common_collocations: aporie dialectique, aporie logique, aporie fruitière, aporie socratique, constituer une aporie
image_search_query: logical dead end contradiction philosophy aporia paradox
image_context: Illustration of intersecting logical paths reaching dead ends representing conceptual contradiction
example_1: Le dialogue s'achève sur une aporie féconde pour la réflexion ultérieure.
example_1_translation: The dialogue ends on a fruitful aporia stimulating further reflection.
example_2: Cette aporie révèle les limites de la raison dans la compréhension de l'infini.
example_2_translation: This aporia reveals the limits of reason in understanding infinity.
example_3: L'aporie aristotélicienne sur la substance demeure irrésolue dans la philosophie médiévale.
example_3_translation: The Aristotelian aporia on substance remains unresolved in medieval philosophy.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: a-po-RI. Stress final: a-po-RI. Rolled 'r' adds French authenticity.
memory_hook: Aporia - from Greek 'a-poros' (without passage/path) - a logical dead end without exit
related_words: aporiétique, dilemme, impasse, paradoxe, antinomie, perplexité
common_mistakes: Avoid anglicized 'ay-POR-ee-uh'; French keeps final stress and softer vowels

primary_meaning: Cassandra; a prophet or person whose warnings of doom are ignored until catastrophe occurs
secondary_meaning: in modern usage, anyone who foresees disaster but is disbelieved by society
usage_notes: Typically used metaphorically: 'jouer les cassandres', 'faire cassandre', 'avertissements de cassandre'. Often feminine.
common_collocations: rôle de cassandre, avertissements de cassandre, crier cassandre, jouer les cassandres, prophétie de cassandre
synonyms: prophète de malheur, Cassandre, oracle ignoré, prophète méconnu
image_search_query: Cassandra Greek mythology prophecy doom warning ignored
image_context: Classical depiction of Cassandra in Greek tragedy surrounded by symbols of ignored prophecy
example_1: Le rôle de cassandre incombe souvent aux analystes économiques lucides.
example_1_translation: The Cassandra role often falls to insightful economic analysts.
example_2: Ses avertissements de cassandre furent ignorés jusqu'à la catastrophe économique de 2008.
example_2_translation: His Cassandra warnings were ignored until the economic catastrophe of 2008.
example_3: Cette scientifique joue cassandre en alertant sur les risques climatiques négligés.
example_3_translation: This scientist plays Cassandra in warning about neglected climate risks.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: ka-SAN-dr. Stress on first 'a', soft 'r' at end.
memory_hook: Cassandre - mythological Trojan princess cursed to speak truth but never be believed
related_words: prophétie, malédiction, tragédie, Oracle de Delphes, destin inexorable
common_mistakes: Avoid treating as verb; use 'jouer cassandre' or 'faire cassandre', not 'cassandrer'

primary_meaning: dendrite, a branched neuronal extension that receives signals from other neurons
secondary_meaning: any tree-like crystalline structure, especially in mineralogy
usage_notes: Used in neuroscience with 'dendrite post-synaptique', 'épine dendritique', 'plasticité dendritique'. Plural: dendrites.
common_collocations: épine dendritique, plasticité dendritique, arbor dendritique, synapse dendritique, transmission dendritique
image_search_query: neuron dendrite branched structure receiving synapses
image_context: Detailed microscopic image of neural dendrites receiving synaptic connections
example_1: Les dendrites permettent la transmission des influx nerveux entre neurones.
example_1_translation: Dendrites enable the transmission of nerve impulses between neurons.
example_2: L'étude des dendrites révèle les mécanismes de la plasticité neuronale et de l'apprentissage.
example_2_translation: The study of dendrites reveals mechanisms of neuronal plasticity and learning.
example_3: La réduction des épines dendritiques dans les maladies neurodégénératives altère la cognition.
example_3_translation: Reduction of dendritic spines in neurodegenerative diseases impairs cognition.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: DEN-drite. Stress on first syllable. Rolled 'r' optional but authentic.
memory_hook: Dendrite - from Greek 'dendron' (tree) + '-ite' (rock/mineral form) - tree-shaped neural branch
related_words: axone, synapse, neurone, soma, potentiel post-synaptique, plasticité
common_mistakes: Confuse with 'axone' (sending branch); dendrite receives, axone sends

primary_meaning: ellipsis, the deliberate omission of words necessary for complete grammatical sense
secondary_meaning: in mathematics, a closed curve formed by a point equidistant from two foci
usage_notes: Rhetorical device common in poetry and advertising. Phrases: 'ellipse rhétorique', 'ellipse syntaxique', 'ellipse pragmatique'
common_collocations: ellipse rhétorique, ellipse syntaxique, ellipse pragmatique, effet d'ellipse, procédé d'ellipse
image_search_query: rhetoric ellipsis omitted words missing elements literary device
image_context: Text with deliberately missing words creating emphasis and artistic effect
example_1: L'ellipse dans 'Jeanne danse' omet le sujet 'elle' pour plus de fluidité poétique.
example_1_translation: The ellipsis in 'Jeanne dances' omits the subject 'she' for poetic fluidity.
example_2: L'ellipse rhétorique crée un effet de suggestion et de suspense narratif.
example_2_translation: The rhetorical ellipsis creates an effect of suggestion and narrative suspense.
example_3: Baudelaire emploie l'ellipse pour intensifier l'émotion dans ses vers.
example_3_translation: Baudelaire employs ellipsis to intensify emotion in his verses.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: e-LIPS. Final 's' silent. Nasal 'e' at beginning.
memory_hook: Ellipse - from Greek 'elleipsis' (falling short, deficiency) - grammatically incomplete but expressive
related_words: zeugme, asyndète, allusion, implicite, sous-entendu, lacune
common_mistakes: Confuse mathematical ellipse (oval) with rhetorical ellipse (omission); they're homonyms

primary_meaning: fatal, disastrous, bringing doom or calamity; having sinister or ominous character
secondary_meaning: in tragedy, pertaining to destiny that leads inevitably to ruin
usage_notes: Literary and formal register. Agrees with noun: 'une décision funeste', 'des conséquences funestes'. Used for events/outcomes.
common_collocations: conséquences funestes, rencontre funeste, destin funeste, décision funeste, influence funeste
synonyms: fatal, calamiteux, désastreux, néfaste, maudit, tragique
image_search_query: dark destiny tragic fate ominous prophecy doom classical tragedy
image_context: Classical tragedy scene depicting fatal moment of destiny leading to ruin
example_1: La rencontre funeste marqua le début de la tragédie inexorable.
example_1_translation: The fateful encounter marked the beginning of inevitable tragedy.
example_2: Les conséquences funestes de cette décision mirent fin à sa carrière politique.
example_2_translation: The fatal consequences of this decision ended his political career.
example_3: L'amour funeste entre les deux ennemis héréditaires provoqua la destruction mutuelle.
example_3_translation: The ill-fated love between hereditary enemies provoked mutual destruction.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: fy-NƐST. Nasal 'u' in French. Stress on final syllable.
memory_hook: Funeste - from Latin 'funestus' (deadly, pertaining to funerals) - carrying death/doom
related_words: fatal, maudit, tragique, néfaste, calamité, destin inexorable
common_mistakes: Not interchangeable with 'funeral'; 'funeste' means cursed/doomed, not sad

primary_meaning: geometry, the mathematical study of shapes, sizes, and spatial relationships
secondary_meaning: the abstract system of spatial relationships and transformations
usage_notes: Distinct sub-fields: 'géométrie euclidienne', 'géométrie non-euclidienne', 'géométrie analytique', 'géométrie projective'
common_collocations: géométrie euclidienne, géométrie non-euclidienne, géométrie analytique, géométrie projective, géométrie riemannienne
synonyms: science de l'espace, étude des formes, science spatiale
image_search_query: geometric shapes mathematics spatial relationships coordinate system
image_context: Complex geometric diagrams and three-dimensional spatial models
example_1: La géométrie non-euclidienne a révolutionné notre compréhension de l'espace et du temps.
example_1_translation: Non-Euclidean geometry revolutionized our understanding of space and time.
example_2: La géométrie analytique permet de résoudre des problèmes spatiaux par le calcul algébrique.
example_2_translation: Analytic geometry allows solving spatial problems through algebraic calculation.
example_3: Riemann a étendu la géométrie au-delà des postulats euclidiens.
example_3_translation: Riemann extended geometry beyond Euclidean postulates.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: je-o-ME-tri. Stress on third syllable. Soft 'j' sound.
memory_hook: Géométrie - from Greek 'geo' (earth) + 'metria' (measurement) - measuring the earth
related_words: géométre, géométral, plan, dimension, topologie, isométrie

primary_meaning: hippopotamus, a massive semi-aquatic African mammal with thick skin and powerful jaws
secondary_meaning: any large, bulky, aggressive animal symbolizing hidden power
usage_notes: Zoological term; also used metaphorically for large, powerful entities. Invariably masculine.
common_collocations: hippopotame fluvial, hippopotame pygmée, comportement d'hippopotame, troupeau d'hippopotames, danger d'hippopotame
synonyms: Hippopotamus amphibius, pachyderme aquatique, géant africain
image_search_query: hippopotamus African river wildlife massive powerful animal
image_context: Large hippopotamus partially submerged in African river with open mouth
example_1: L'hippopotame passe la majorité de sa journée dans l'eau pour se rafraîchir.
example_1_translation: The hippopotamus spends most of the day in water to cool itself.
example_2: L'hippopotame est considéré comme l'un des animaux les plus dangereux d'Afrique.
example_2_translation: The hippopotamus is considered one of Africa's most dangerous animals.
example_3: Le troupeau d'hippopotames défend férocement son territoire contre les intrus.
example_3_translation: The hippopotamus herd fiercely defends its territory against intruders.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: i-PO-po-tam. Stress on first 'o'. Double 'p' matters.

primary_meaning: iconoclastic, attacking or rejecting cherished beliefs, traditions, or cultural icons
secondary_meaning: historically, regarding the destruction of religious images; modern: any rebel against established norms
usage_notes: Can be noun (m/f) or adjective. Political, artistic, religious contexts. 'Pensée iconoclaste', 'artiste iconoclaste'
common_collocations: pensée iconoclaste, artiste iconoclaste, tendance iconoclaste, mouvement iconoclaste, rôle iconoclaste
image_search_query: breaking religious icons cultural rebellion destroying idols protest
image_context: Historical and modern depictions of breaking sacred images or challenging traditions
example_1: L'artiste iconoclaste remet en question toutes les conventions établies de la tradition.
example_1_translation: The iconoclastic artist questions all established traditions' conventions.
example_2: La pensée iconoclaste remise en cause les dogmes traditionnels de la religion.
example_2_translation: Iconoclastic thought challenges the traditional dogmas of religion.
example_3: Les réformes iconoclastes du 16e siècle transformèrent le paysage religieux européen.
example_3_translation: The iconoclastic reforms of the 16th century transformed the European religious landscape.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: i-ko-no-KLAST. Stress on final syllable.
related_words: iconoclasme, hérésie, révolte, transgression, révolution culturelle

primary_meaning: jeremiad, a long lamentation or complaint; a denunciation of vice and folly
secondary_meaning: in literature, an extended elegy or expression of sorrow and disapproval
usage_notes: Literary and formal. Often plural in context. 'Débiter des jérémiades', 'chaîne de jérémiades'
common_collocations: jérémiades sans fin, débiter des jérémiades, chaîne de jérémiades, prophétique jérémiade
image_search_query: lamentation weeping sorrow complaint prophetic condemnation biblical
image_context: Classical artistic depiction of weeping prophet figure delivering lament
example_1: Ses jérémiades incessantes ne font qu'aggraver l'atmosphère morose du groupe.
example_1_translation: His endless jeremiads only worsen the gloomy atmosphere of the group.
example_2: Le poète excelle dans les jérémiades sur la vanité des grandeurs humaines.
example_2_translation: The poet excels in jeremiads about the vanity of human greatness.
example_3: Les jérémiades de Jérémie annonçaient la chute de Jérusalem.
example_3_translation: Jeremiah's jeremiads announced the fall of Jerusalem.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: je-re-mi-AD. Stress on final syllable. Soft 'j' sound.
memory_hook: Jérémiade - from prophet Jeremiah's lamentations over Jerusalem's fall
related_words: lamentation, élégie, plainte, gémissement, complainte, prophétie
common_mistakes: Not singular 'jérémiade' alone; usually plural 'jérémiades' in usage

primary_meaning: Kafkaesque, resembling the nightmarish bureaucracy or existential anxiety found in Kafka's works
secondary_meaning: characterized by absurdity, helplessness, and oppressive systems defying rational explanation
usage_notes: Adjective only, modifying nouns. 'Atmosphère kafkaïenne', 'bureaucratie kafkaïenne', 'absurdité kafkaïenne'
common_collocations: atmosphère kafkaïenne, bureaucratie kafkaïenne, absurdité kafkaïenne, angoisse kafkaïenne, sens kafkaïen
image_search_query: Kafkaesque nightmare bureaucratic labyrinth absurd system oppression
image_context: Abstract visual representation of bureaucratic nightmare and existential anxiety
example_1: La bureaucratie kafkaïenne étouffe toute initiative individuelle et créative.
example_1_translation: Kafkaesque bureaucracy stifles every individual and creative initiative.
example_2: Le roman présente une atmosphère kafkaïenne profondément désorientante et anxiogène.
example_2_translation: The novel presents a Kafkaesque atmosphere profoundly disorienting and anxiety-inducing.
example_3: Dans cette absurdité kafkaïenne, le protagoniste lutte contre des forces invisibles.
example_3_translation: In this Kafkaesque absurdity, the protagonist struggles against invisible forces.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: kaf-ka-JE. Nasal ending. Stress on final 'i'.
memory_hook: Kafkaïen - from author Franz Kafka's depiction of alienated modern existence
common_mistakes: Maintain diaeresis on 'ï' to keep two-vowel pronunciation; 'kafkaien' alone sounds wrong

primary_meaning: lability, the quality of being unstable, changeable, or emotionally variable
secondary_meaning: in chemistry/physics, the readiness with which a substance undergoes change
usage_notes: Used in psychology ('labilité émotionnelle'), chemistry, economics. Technical register.
common_collocations: labilité émotionnelle, labilité d'humeur, labilité du marché, labilité émotionnelle
image_search_query: emotional instability fluctuating moods unstable variable changeable
image_context: Abstract visual representation of fluctuating emotional states and instability
example_1: La labilité émotionnelle caractérise certains troubles psychologiques comme le trouble bipolaire.
example_1_translation: Emotional lability characterizes certain psychological disorders like bipolar disorder.
example_2: La labilité du marché financier reflète l'incertitude économique ambiante.
example_2_translation: The lability of financial markets reflects surrounding economic uncertainty.
example_3: Cette labilité neurochimique explique la sensibilité excessive aux stimuli externes.
example_3_translation: This neurochemical lability explains excessive sensitivity to external stimuli.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: la-bi-li-TE. Stress on final syllable.
memory_hook: Labilité - from Latin 'labilis' (slipping, sliding) - tendency to slip/change
common_mistakes: Avoid anglicized 'lab-IL-i-tee'; French keeps stress on final 'té'

primary_meaning: misalliance, an unsuitable or improper marriage, especially between unequal social ranks
secondary_meaning: any alliance or association considered inappropriate or beneath one's status
usage_notes: Formal/literary context. Historical term; modern usage often ironic or antiquated.
common_collocations: mésalliance de classes, mésalliance matrimoniale, tomber en mésalliance, accusation de mésalliance
synonyms: union inappropriée, mariage indigne, alliance désavantageuse
antonyms: mariage convenable, union appropriée, alliance prestigieuse
image_search_query: unsuitable marriage class difference wedding social mismatch
image_context: Historical depiction of conflicting social classes in marriage scenario
example_1: La mésalliance fut source de tensions familiales prolongées et d'ostracisme social.
example_1_translation: The misalliance was a source of prolonged family tensions and social ostracism.
example_2: Cette mésalliance de classes sociales choquait profondément la haute société.
example_2_translation: This misalliance of social classes deeply shocked high society.
example_3: Elle surmonta les préjugés contre cette mésalliance considérée scandaleuse.
example_3_translation: She overcame prejudices against this misalliance considered scandalous.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: me-za-LIANS. Stress on final 's'. Nasal 'a'.
related_words: alliance, mariage, classe sociale, inégalité, préjugé
common_mistakes: Not 'mésalliance' used as verb; it's noun only

primary_meaning: nepotism, favoritism shown to relatives or friends in professional or political appointments
secondary_meaning: the broader practice of using power to benefit family members regardless of merit
usage_notes: Formal/political register. Often with 'accusation de', 'preuve de', 'système de'
common_collocations: accusation de népotisme, système de népotisme, preuve de népotisme, épidémie de népotisme
image_search_query: nepotism favoritism family political corruption bias
image_context: Illustration of political corruption and unfair advantage given to family members
example_1: Le népotisme dans l'attribution des marchés publics a provoqué un scandale majeur.
example_1_translation: Nepotism in public contract awards caused a major scandal.
example_2: L'accusation de népotisme a entraîné la démission inévitable du ministre.
example_2_translation: The accusation of nepotism led to the minister's inevitable resignation.
example_3: Le système de népotisme perpétue les inégalités sociales et économiques.
example_3_translation: The system of nepotism perpetuates social and economic inequalities.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: ne-PO-tis-m. Stress on second syllable.
memory_hook: Népotisme - from Latin 'nepos' (grandson/nephew) - favoritism toward relatives

primary_meaning: obsession or preoccupation that clouds judgment or mental clarity
secondary_meaning: a state of mental confusion or obscuration caused by fixed thoughts
usage_notes: Formal/medical register. Often with 'obnubilation par', 'état d'obnubilation'
common_collocations: obnubilation par, état d'obnubilation, obnubilation mentale, obnubilation du jugement
image_search_query: obsession fixation clouded mind mental fog confusion preoccupation
image_context: Abstract visualization of clouded thinking and mental preoccupation
example_1: L'obnubilation par la réussite professionnelle mène souvent à l'épuisement professionnel.
example_1_translation: Obsession with professional success often leads to professional burnout.
example_2: Son obnubilation du passé l'empêchait complètement de profiter du présent.
example_2_translation: His obsession with the past completely prevented him from enjoying the present.
example_3: Cette obnubilation mentale affectait sa capacité à prendre des décisions rationnelles.
example_3_translation: This mental preoccupation affected his ability to make rational decisions.
pronunciation_tips: Five syllables: ob-nu-bi-la-SION. Stress on final syllable. Nasal 'on'.
memory_hook: Obnubilation - from Latin 'obnubilare' (to cloud over) - mental clouds/fog
